frameworks.name: theory of mind
frameworks.one_line: children infer mental causes of others' actions to predict behaviour

frameworks.name: habit vs goal-directed control
frameworks.one_line: dual-system distinction between reinforced habits and flexible goal pursuit

frameworks.name: reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: habits framed as outcomes of long-period reinforcement on action policies

core_question: Do children come to understand that other agents can act habitually and thus persist in suboptimal behaviour even when they 'know better'?
key_assumptions: habits and goal-directed behaviour can be dissociated when optimal policy changes without reinforcement
children's predictions of others' actions index their underlying mentalising model
habit attribution is a distinct competence from belief/desire reasoning
manipulations: scenarios where habitual policy diverges from currently optimal action
key_findings: all tested children predicted agents would act consistently with their goals
between ages 5 and 10, children showed increasing understanding that habits can cause persistent suboptimal action
interpretations: social reasoning development includes learning that people's actions can be suboptimal despite 'knowing better'
habit attribution extends ToM beyond belief- and expectation-based accounts of action prediction
